claim: The Young's modulus of the present steel is 225 GPa.
evidence_text: preserving a high Young's modulus of 225 GPa

claim: Synchrotron XRD experiments at SSRF beamline BL02U2 used an energy of 21.7 keV (wavelength 0.5714 Å) and beam size 0.5 mm × 1 mm; dislocation density was calculated using the modified Williamson-Hall method.
evidence_text: The energy of the synchrotron X-ray was 21.7 keV, corresponding to a wavelength of 0.5714 A. The beam size was 0.5 mm 1 mm. ... The modified Williamson-Hall (MWH) method was employed to calculate dislocation density.

claim: TEM specimens were prepared by double-jet electro-polishing in a solution of 12.5 vol.% perchloric acid and 87.5 vol.% ethanol at −25 °C and 30 V.
evidence_text: disks... were prepared by double-jet electro-polishing using a solution of 12.5 vol. % perchloric acid and 87.5 vol. % ethanol at -25 °C and 30 V.

claim: The volume fraction of TiB2 was quantitatively measured using ImageJ software.
evidence_text: The volume fraction of TiB2 was quantitatively analyzed by using ImageJ software.

claim: The present steel achieves a high specific modulus (E/ρ) by combining a low density of 6.88 g cm−3 and a high Young's modulus of 225 GPa.
evidence_text: this new low-density steel achieves an impressively low density of 6.88 g cm−3 while preserving a high Young's modulus of 225 GPa, resulting in a high specific modulus.

claim: Synchrotron X-ray diffraction (s-XRD) was performed in reflection mode at beamline BL02U2 in SSRF with an X-ray energy of 21.7 keV (wavelength 0.5714 Å) and a beam size of 0.5 mm × 1 mm; the modified Williamson-Hall (MWH) method with LaB6 instrumental correction was used to calculate dislocation density.
evidence_text: The energy of the synchrotron X-ray was 21.7 keV, corresponding to a wavelength of 0.5714 Å. The beam size was 0.5 mm × 1 mm. ... The modified Williamson-Hall (MWH) method was employed to calculate dislocation density. The instrumental profile of LaB6 from the same synchrotron facility was used.

claim: A two-dimensional representative volume element (RVE) based on the EBSD result was constructed with a side length of 200 μm and a mesh size of 0.4 μm yielding 500×500 elements; periodic boundary conditions were applied to the RVE model.
evidence_text: our RVE dimensions match the actual side length of 200 μm for the selected EBSD image. ... we employed a mesh size of 0.4 μm that corresponds to 500 × 500 mesh elements. ... periodic boundary conditions were applied to the boundary nodes of the RVE model

claim: The present low-density steel exhibits a yield strength σ_y of 503 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength σ_u of 672 MPa, and a uniform elongation of 13%, compared to 389 MPa, 549 MPa, and 11% for the conventional Fe-TiB2 steel.
evidence_text: the present steel exhibits attractive mechanical properties, including σ_y of 503 MPa and σ_u of 672 MPa, which are 22 % and 30 % more than the values of the existing Fe-TiB2 steel (with σ_y of 389 MPa and σ_u of 549 MPa). Notably, the uniform elongation (13 %) of the present steel is even higher than that (11 %) of the existing Fe-TiB2 steel

claim: The present steel has a density of 6.88 g cm−3, a Young's modulus of 225 GPa, a specific modulus of 32.7 GPa g−1 cm3, a specific yield strength (SYS) of 73.1 MPa g−1 cm3, and a specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) of 97.7 MPa g−1 cm3.
evidence_text: The ρ of the present steel is 6.88 g cm−3... Its E value of 225 GPa... the specific modulus (E/ρ) of the present steel is calculated to be 32.7 GPa g−1 cm3... the present steel has high specific yield strength (SYS) and specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) of 73.1 and 97.7 MPa g−1 cm3, respectively.

claim: In the SYS versus uniform elongation map, the data point of the present steel represents one of the optimal mechanical properties among existing ceramic-reinforcement and Al-added low-density steels.
evidence_text: the SYS versus UE of the present steel is compared with those of existing lightweight steels... The data point of the present steel represents one of the optimal mechanical properties in the SYS-UE space.

claim: Fig. 3 presents (a) tensile engineering stress-strain curves comparing the present steel, conventional Fe-TiB2 steel, and interstitial-free steel; (b) a property map of specific yield strength versus uniform elongation; and (c) a map of specific modulus versus SYS×UE for lightweight steels.
evidence_text: Fig. 3. Physical and mechanical properties of the present steel in comparison with other lightweight steels... (a) A typical tensile engineering stress-strain curve... (b) The property map summarizing the uniform elongation (UE) and specific yield strength (SYS)... (c) The property map summarizes the key material parameters for lightweight performance, including the specific Young’s modulus and the mechanical properties indicated by the product of specific yield strength and uniform elongation.

claim: Fig. 4 shows the initial microstructure of the present steel including a pseudo 3D SEM image, high-magnification SEM views with phase delineation, XRD patterns, and computerized tomography-based spatial and size distributions of particles (number and volume fractions).
evidence_text: Fig. 4. Initial microstructure of the present steel and statistical results of particle size distributions: (a) Representative pseudo 3D SEM image of surface in three different directions. (b) High magnification SEM image at the RD... (c) High-magnification SEM image with dashed red lines delineating the Mn-rich austenite and Al-rich ferrite regions. (d) XRD patterns. (e) Spatial distribution of particles in different size domains, measured by computerized tomography. Particle size distribution in terms of number fractions (f) and volume fractions (g), respectively.

claim: The area fraction of TiB2 particles is 9.6% on RD surface and 13.4% on ND surface, consistent with the volume fraction of 12.5% measured by XRD.
evidence_text: The area fraction of particles ... is estimated to be 9.6 % from the RD surface and 13.4 % from the ND surface, in accordance with the volume fraction of 12.5 % measured by XRD.

claim: TiB2 particle sizes range from 2.4 to 35 μm with an average size of approximately 15 μm.
evidence_text: The size of TiB2 particles falls within 2.4–35 μm, with an average value of ∼15 μm.

claim: Two types of TiB2 particles exist: irregularly shaped small particles (few μm, plate-like or needle-like, debris of broken eutectic) and regularly shaped coarse hexagonal prism particles (tens of μm, primary).
evidence_text: Two types of TiB particles can be identified ... The first type corresponds to the irregularly shaped, relatively small TiB2 particles with an average size of only a few micrometers. These small particles are either plate-like or needle-like ... The second type consists of the regularly shaped, coarse particles with an average size of tens of micrometers. These large particles are mostly hexagonal prisms

claim: Both large primary and small eutectic TiB2 particles are exclusively located at grain boundaries and not within matrix grain interiors, with sizes ≥0.9 μm resolvable in EBSD.
evidence_text: Both large primary TiB2 particles and smaller eutectic TiB2 particles are exclusively in the submicron/micron-scale range (≥0.9 μm; resolvable in EBSD, Fig. S3(d)) and located at grain boundaries, with no TiB2 particles observed within the interior of the matrix grains.

claim: Micro-CT shows small particles (0.8–5.0 μm) predominate in number, but coarse particles (10.0–42.7 μm) account for over 80% of the total particle area fraction.
evidence_text: Micro-CT analysis (Fig. 4(e–g)) reveals that while small particles (0.8–5.0 μm) prevail in numbers, coarse particles (10.0–42.7 μm) govern the microstructure by accounting for >80 % of the total area fraction of particles.

claim: EBSD area fractions are approximately 59% austenite, 28% ferrite, and 13% TiB2, consistent with XRD volume fractions of 62%, 26%, and 12.5% respectively.
evidence_text: The area fractions of the austenite, ferrite, and TiB2 particles are estimated from the EBSD result to be around 59 %, 28 % and 13 %, respectively, in accordance with their volume fractions (62 %, 26 % and 12.5 %, respectively) obtained by XRD analysis

claim: The matrix exhibits a banded microstructure of alternating δ-ferrite lamellae and austenite; average ferrite band thickness is ~4.3 μm and austenite layer thickness is 12.1 μm.
evidence_text: The average thickness of ferrite bands is ∼4.3 μm, smaller than that of austenite layers (12.1 μm).

claim: δ-ferrite grains are elongated with an average size of 4.4 μm, while austenite grains are mostly equiaxed with an average size of ~3.9 μm.
evidence_text: These δ-ferrite grains have an average size of 4.4 μm (the inset in Fig. 5(d3)). By contrast, the austenite grains are mostly equiaxed with a larger average size of ∼3.9 μm (Fig. 5(b3)).

claim: KAM mapping reveals non-uniform strain distribution: δ-ferrite lamellae/grains possess higher KAM values (indicating higher dislocation density) than most austenite grains.
evidence_text: The KAM mapping ... reveals a non-uniform strain distribution in the matrix. Specifically, the ferrite lamellae and the corresponding grains possess higher KAM values (green color) than most austenite grains, in which no evident accumulations of misorientations can be observed.

claim: Austenite grains with GOS<2° (fully recrystallized) constitute 90.8% area fraction of austenite, whereas only 52.2% of δ-ferrite is recrystallized.
evidence_text: The austenite grains with GOS<2°, which can be considered as fully recrystallized, account for 90.8 % (area fraction) of the austenite phase. In comparison, the recrystallized δ-ferrite accounts for only 52.2 % of the ferrite phase (Fig. 5(c3)).

claim: Weak-beam TEM reveals dislocation tangles at the δ-ferrite/TiB2 interface, with higher density than at austenite/TiB2 interfaces.
evidence_text: Weak beam bright-field TEM (Fig. 6(a2)) reveals detailed dislocation tangles at the δ-ferrite/TiB interface. ... At austenite/TiB2 interfaces ... weak beam bright-field images (Fig. 6(b2)) show substantially lower dislocation densities than observed at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces.

claim: Selected-area diffraction confirms a crystallographic orientation relationship at the small TiB2/δ-ferrite interface: {110}_δ-ferrite || {1-101}_TiB2 and <111>_δ-ferrite || <11-20>_TiB2, suggesting a coherent or semi-coherent interface.
evidence_text: The superimposed selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP) ... confirms a crystallographic orientation relationship at this interface: {110}_δ-ferrite || {1-101}_TiB2 and <111>_δ-ferrite || <11-20>_TiB2. This relationship suggests a coherent or semi-coherent interface

claim: No clear crystallographic orientation relationship is observed at interfaces involving δ-ferrite/large TiB2 particles or austenite/TiB2.
evidence_text: SADP analysis (Fig. S4(a–a3, b–b2)) revealed no clear crystallographic orientation relationship for interfaces involving either δ-ferrite/large TiB2 particles or austenite/TiB2.

claim: Dislocation tangles occur at all TiB2/matrix interfaces regardless of particle size, consistent with their exclusive grain boundary location.
evidence_text: Notably, dislocation tangles occur at all TiB2/matrix interfaces regardless of particle size, consistent with the exclusive grain boundary distribution of the TiB2 particles.

claim: Weak beam bright-field TEM reveals detailed dislocation tangles at the δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface.
evidence_text: Weak beam bright-field TEM (Fig. 6(a₂)) reveals detailed dislocation tangles at the δ-ferrite/TiB interface.

claim: Weak beam bright-field images show substantially lower dislocation densities at austenite/TiB₂ interfaces than at δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interfaces.
evidence_text: weak beam bright-field images (Fig. 6(b₂)) show substantially lower dislocation densities than observed at δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interfaces.

claim: No clear crystallographic orientation relationship is observed for interfaces involving δ-ferrite/large TiB₂ particles or austenite/TiB₂.
evidence_text: SADP analysis (Fig. S4(a–a₃, b–b₂)) revealed no clear crystallographic orientation relationship for interfaces involving either δ-ferrite/large TiB₂ particles or austenite/TiB₂.

claim: Dislocation tangles occur at all TiB₂/matrix interfaces regardless of particle size, consistent with the exclusive grain boundary distribution of TiB₂ particles.
evidence_text: Notably, dislocation tangles occur at all TiB₂/matrix interfaces regardless of particle size, consistent with the exclusive grain boundary distribution of the TiB2 particles.

claim: The volume fractions of austenite and δ-ferrite remain nearly constant after tensile deformation to fracture, indicating no martensitic phase transformation.
evidence_text: The comparison between the phase maps before and after tensile deformation (Figs. 5(a₁) and 7(a₁)) reveals that the volume fractions of austenite and δ-ferrite remain nearly constant during the entire deformation process, indicating that no phase transformation from austenite to martensite occurred.

claim: KAM mapping of the deformed microstructure shows defect accumulation in both δ-ferrite and austenite, in contrast to the initial microstructure where defects existed predominantly in δ-ferrite.
evidence_text: Fig. 7(a₂) shows the KAM mapping of the deformed microstructure, where defects accumulate in both δ-ferrite and austenite. This is in stark contrast to the initial microstructure (Fig. 5(a₂)), where defects exist predominantly in the δ-ferrite.

claim: After tensile deformation, both δ-ferrite and austenite grains away from TiB₂ particles exhibit dense dislocation tangles, contrasting with individually identified dislocations in the undeformed samples.
evidence_text: In both δ-ferrite (Fig. 7(a₁)) and austenite (Fig. 7(a₂)) grains away from TiB₂ particles, dense dislocation tangles were observed, contrasting sharply with the individually identified dislocations in the undeformed samples (Fig. 6).

claim: Austenite exhibited the most pronounced increase in dislocation density after straining, as evidenced by highly compact tangles where dislocation lines are undistinguished in weak-beam bright-field TEM.
evidence_text: Notably, the austenite exhibited the most pronounced increase in dislocation density after straining, as evidenced by the highly compact tangles where dislocation lines are undistinguished in weak-beam bright-field TEM (Fig. 7(c₂)).

claim: The first defect-containing TiB₂ particle fractures occurred at true plastic strain ε_p = 0.0069 and applied true stress σ_a = 556 MPa, exceeding the yield stress σ_0.2 = 503 MPa.
evidence_text: The first defect-containing particle fractures (the red arrow in Fig. 8(a₁)) occurred at the true plastic strain ε_p = 0.0069 and the applied true stress σ_a = 556 MPa (exceeding the yield stress σ_0.2 = 503 MPa).

claim: Larger TiB₂ particles experienced elevated stress concentrations, increasing their susceptibility to cracking, with the largest particles (>10 μm) constituting over 80% of the TiB₂ volume fraction and dominating bulk mechanical behavior.
evidence_text: Larger particles experienced elevated stress concentrations, increasing their susceptibility to cracking... the largest particles (> 10 μm) dominated the microstructure, constituting > 80 % of the volume fraction of TiB₂ and thus dictating bulk mechanical behavior.

claim: Multiple cracking observed in some TiB₂ particles confirms persistent load transfer between matrix and particles following initial fracture.
evidence_text: multiple cracking observed in some TiB particles... confirms persistent load transfer between matrix and particles following initial fracture.

claim: Statistical analysis of 336 TiB₂ particles (189 predominantly austenite-contacting, 147 primarily ferrite-contacting) identified TiB₂ fracture as a major damage source; austenite-surrounded particles exhibited significantly higher fracture frequencies due to multi-directional shear strains from single-slip activation in individual austenite grains.
evidence_text: Statistical analysis of 336 TiB2 particles, including 189 predominantly austenite-contacting particles and 147 primarily ferrite-contacting ones... identify TiB2 fracture as a major damage source in LDS... austenite-surrounded particles exhibited significantly higher fracture frequencies. This is attributed to their encapsulation by multiple austenite grains... where single-slip activation in individual grains subjects TiB particles to multi-directional shear strains

claim: Voids near the fracture surface result from cracking of large primary TiB₂ particles; cracks are oriented nearly perpendicular to the tensile axis, suggesting stress-driven fracture due to increased load-bearing with larger sizes.
evidence_text: numerous voids that result from the cracking of large primary TiB2 particles... The cracks are oriented nearly perpendicular to the tensile axis, suggesting that the fracture of large primary particles is stress-driven due to increased load-bearing with larger sizes.

claim: Fractography demonstrates a mixed fracture mode comprising ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage fractures of TiB₂ particles, and particle/matrix decohesion.
evidence_text: Fractography (Fig. 8(c, c₁)) further demonstrates a mixed fracture mode, comprising ductile dimples (yellow arrows), quasi-cleavage fractures of TiB₂ particles (blue arrows), and particle/matrix decohesion (red arrows).

claim: After tensile fracture, numerous voids form from the cracking of large primary TiB2 particles, which crack perpendicular to the tensile axis in a stress-driven manner, while smaller particles generally remain intact.
evidence_text: Post-fracture analysis near the fracture surface (Fig. 8(b–b2)) revealed numerous voids that result from the cracking of large primary TiB2 particles, as indicated by the blue arrows in Fig. 8(b–b2). In contrast, smaller particles generally remain intact. The cracks are oriented nearly perpendicular to the tensile axis, suggesting that the fracture of large primary particles is stress-driven due to increased load-bearing with larger sizes.

claim: Substantial particle/matrix decohesion is observed near the fracture surface.
evidence_text: we observed substantial particle/matrix decohesion, as indicated by the red arrows in Fig. 8(b1, b2).

claim: The fracture surface exhibits a mixed fracture mode consisting of ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage fractures of TiB2 particles, and particle/matrix decohesion.
evidence_text: Fractography (Fig. 8(c, c1)) further demonstrates a mixed fracture mode, comprising ductile dimples (yellow arrows), quasi-cleavage fractures of TiB2 particles (blue arrows), and particle/matrix decohesion (red arrows).

claim: Correlative μ-DIC measurements show that strain localizations maintain a multiregional distribution aligned at approximately 45° to the loading axis.
evidence_text: Strain localizations maintained a multiregional distribution rather than concentrating continuously and rapidly in a single area (Fig. 9(b–f)). These strain-concentrated regions aligned at approximately 45° to the loading axis.

claim: High local strain of 10% is concentrated at the small TiB2/δ-ferrite interface.
evidence_text: Detailed strain profile analysis revealed high strain (10%) concentrated at the small TiB2/δ-ferrite interface (Fig. 9(g)).

claim: Elevated local strain of 16% is observed at austenite/TiB2 phase boundaries.
evidence_text: Fig. 9(h) illustrates strain distribution in the matrix surrounding TiB particles of varying sizes, showing elevated strain (16%) at austenite/TiB2 phase boundaries.

claim: At higher plastic deformation without particle cracking, strain concentrations of ~20% occur near sharp TiB2/austenite and TiB2/ferrite boundaries.
evidence_text: At higher plastic deformation without cracking, strain concentrations (~20%) were also observed near sharp TiB2/austenite and TiB2/ferrite boundaries (green arrows, Fig. 9(a) and corresponding Fig. 9(i)).

claim: Local strains in the matrix adjacent to TiB2 particles significantly exceed those farther from the particles, indicating that TiB2 particles effectively promote plastic deformation in the surrounding matrix.
evidence_text: local strains in the matrix (austenite or δ-ferrite) adjacent to TiB2 particles significantly exceeded those farther from the particles (between the two dashed lines in Fig. 9(i)), indicating that TiB2 particles effectively promote plastic deformation in the surrounding matrix.

claim: Synchrotron X-ray diffraction identifies three phases: austenite with lattice parameter a≈0.368 nm, δ-ferrite with a≈0.295 nm, and TiB2 with a≈0.309 nm and c≈0.328 nm.
evidence_text: three phases were identified: austenite (with lattice parameter a≈0.368 nm), ferrite (a≈0.295 nm), and TiB2 (a≈0.309 nm, c≈0.328 nm).

claim: For ferrite the elastic constants are C11=273 GPa, C12=110 GPa, C44=82 GPa; for austenite they are C11=169 GPa, C12=82 GPa, C44=96 GPa.
evidence_text: At room temperature, the elastic constants for ferrite are taken as C11 = 273 GPa, C12 = 110 GPa, and C44 = 82 GPa [63]. For austenite, the corresponding values are C11 = 169 GPa, C12 = 82 GPa, and C44 = 96 GPa [64].

claim: In the as-hot-rolled condition, the dislocation density of δ-ferrite is (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m−2 and that of austenite is (3±0.7)×10^14 m−2, as measured by synchrotron XRD MWH analysis.
evidence_text: the initial dislocation density of ferrite is (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m−2, surpassing the value of (3±0.7)×10^14 m−2 for austenite.

claim: After tensile deformation, the dislocation density of δ-ferrite increases slightly to (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m−2, while that of austenite increases by an order of magnitude to (2±0.3)×10^15 m−2.
evidence_text: the dislocation density of ferrite experiences a slight increase, reaching (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m−2. By contrast, the dislocation density of austenite shows a significant rise by an order of magnitude, reaching (2±0.3)×10^15 m−2.

claim: Orowan bypass mechanism is inapplicable to the present steel because TiB2 particles are micron-sized (2.4–35 μm, average 15 μm), located exclusively at grain or phase boundaries, and coarse particles (>10 μm) dominate the volume fraction; applying the Orowan equation would overestimate TiB2 strengthening.
evidence_text: The Orowan bypass mechanism is only applicable for nanoscale precipitates dispersed within grain interiors. In contrast, the TiB2 particles in our steel are submicron to coarse micron-sized (2.4-35 μm, average 15 μm) and exclusively located at grain or phase boundaries (Section 3.2, Figs. 4, 5, and S3). Micro-CT analysis further confirmed that coarse particles (> 10 μm) dominate the microstructure by volume fraction.

claim: The flow stress of both ferrite and austenite phases is decomposed as σ = σ₀ + σ_gb + σ_dis, where σ₀ is lattice friction stress, σ_gb follows the Hall–Petch relation σ_gb = k_HP/√d, and σ_dis is given by the Taylor equation σ_dis = α M μ b √ρ.
evidence_text: In terms of the metallic matrix, the plastic flow of ferrite and austenite phases follows the Von Mises yield criterion. Considering the strain hardening of the two phases, their flow stress σ can be decomposed into several parts as σ = σ_0 + σ_gb + σ_dis (4) where σ_0 is the lattice friction stress, σ_gb represents grain boundary strengthening, and σ_dis is dislocation strengthening. The grainboundary strengthening follows the Hall-Petch relation as σ_gb = k_HP/√d (5) ... The dislocation hardening σ_dis is a function of dislocation density and described by the Taylor equation [68] as σ_dis = α M μ b √ρ (6)

claim: Dislocation density evolution during tensile straining follows the Kocks-Mecking model dρ/dε = M (k₁ √ρ − k₂ ρ), where k₁ is the athermal storage constant and k₂ is the dynamic recovery factor; k₂ remains constant at room temperature and constant strain rate.
evidence_text: The evolution of dislocation density during tensile straining is described by the Kocks-Mecking model [69–71] as dρ/dε = M (k1 √ρ - k2 ρ) (7) where ε represents the true strain, k1 is a constant associated with the athermal storage of the moving dislocations, and k2 is the dynamic recovery factor influenced by thermal activation. Since the tensile tests were performed at room temperature and a constant strain rate, k2 remains a constant value throughout the tensile straining process.

claim: The brittle fracture of TiB2 particles is described by a Weibull distribution, and particle/matrix interfaces are simulated with zero-thickness COH3D8 cohesive elements obeying a bilinear traction-separation law calibrated against μ-DIC experiments.
evidence_text: the brittle fracture strength of TiB2 particles was characterized using the Weibull distribution; specific parameters [72] are provided in Table 1. To accurately simulate the micromechanical behavior of the low-density steel, we explicitly modeled the ferrite-TiB2 and austenite-TiB2 interfaces using zero-thickness COH3D8 cohesive elements governed by a bilinear traction-separation (T-S) law. The debonding parameters for this law, calibrated against μ-DIC experimental results, are also given in Table 1.

claim: The volume fraction of damaged TiB2 particles evolves as f_d* = f₀ (1 − exp(−k ((σ_eq − σ_c)/σ_c)^n)) + f_d0, where σ_c is the critical fracture stress, f₀ is the total proportion of potentially damageable particles, and f_d0 is the initially damaged fraction.
evidence_text: The fracture criterion obeys a Weibull-type law, and the current volume fraction of damaged particles is described by f_d* = f_0 (1 - exp^{-k ((σ_eq - σ_c)/σ_c)^n}) + f_d0 (8) where f_0 is the total proportion of potentially damageable particles, f_d0 is the proportion of initially damaged particles, σ_c is the particle critical fracture stress, σ_eq is the equivalent Von Mises stress in the undamaged particles, and n and k describe the process of progressive damage.

claim: For α-Fe, Young's modulus E=210 GPa, Poisson ratio ν=0.3, lattice friction stress σ₀=150 MPa, Taylor factor M=3.16, athermal storage factor k₁=180,000, dynamic recovery factor k₂=6, initial dislocation density ρ₀=7×10^13 m⁻², shear modulus μ=80.7 GPa, Burgers vector b=0.248 nm, dislocation interaction constant α=0.2, grain size d=8 μm, Hall-Petch slope k_HP=130 MPa μm^1/2.
evidence_text: Table 1 summarizes the values of parameters for simulating both the present steel and the conventional Fe-TiB steel. The material parameters, including E, v, μ, b, α, M, and kHP, are sourced from existing literature [73–78].

claim: δ-ferrite parameters: E=210 GPa, σ₀=150 MPa, k₁=230,000, k₂=6, ρ₀=1.1×10^15 m⁻², d=4.4 μm, k_HP=130 MPa μm^1/2. Austenite parameters: E=163.8 GPa, σ₀=149 MPa, k₁=200,000, k₂=4.5, ρ₀=3×10^14 m⁻², d=3.9 μm, k_HP=460 MPa μm^1/2.
evidence_text: Table 1 summarizes the values of parameters for simulating both the present steel and the conventional Fe-TiB steel. The material parameters, including E, v, μ, b, α, M, and kHP, are sourced from existing literature [73–78].

claim: TiB2 is modeled as linearly elastic with E=565 GPa and ν=0.11; damage parameters include total potentially damageable particle fraction f₀=0.1480, initially damaged fraction f_d0=0.1245, critical fracture stress σ_c=320 MPa, shape factor n=1.5, scale parameter k=0.09; interface stiffness K=10⁶ MPa mm⁻¹, interface strength T_c = 700 MPa (α/δ) and 640 MPa (γ).
evidence_text: Table 1 summarizes the values of parameters for simulating both the present steel and the conventional Fe-TiB steel. The material parameters, including E, v, μ, b, α, M, and kHP, are sourced from existing literature [73–78].

claim: A second RVE containing identical TiB2 distributions but with only α-ferrite matrix was used to simulate conventional Fe-TiB2 steel; the predicted tensile curves for Fe-TiB2 (orange) and interstitial-free steel (black) are consistent with experimental results, demonstrating the reliability of the FE approach.
evidence_text: we prepared a second RVE, which contains identical TiB distributions and boundary conditions (Fig. 2) but consists solely of α- ferrite. This second RVE is used to simulate the mechanical properties of the conventional Fe-TiB steel. ... Based on the parameters for α-ferrite, we can predict the tensile mechanical response for both the conventional Fe-TiB2 (orange curve) and the interstitialfree steel (black curve, which can be treated as the α-ferrite matrix without TiB2), in consistency with experimental results, as shown in Fig. 11(a). Such consistency demonstrates the reliability of our finite element modeling approach.

claim: The present steel achieves a Young's modulus of approximately 225 GPa, surpassing all existing steels without ceramic phases, due to the incorporation of 12.5 vol% TiB2 reinforcement.
evidence_text: the incorporation of 12.5 vol.% TiB2 reinforcement effectively raises the Young’s modulus E to approximately 225 GPa—surpassing that of all existing steels without ceramic phases.

claim: The density of the present steel is approximately 6.88 g cm^−3, similar to Fe-Mn-Al-C type low-density steels with 8–10 wt.% Al, resulting in a specific modulus of 32.7 GPa cm^3 g^−1.
evidence_text: the addition of ∼4 wt. % Al, together with light ceramic particles, results in a remarkably low density of ∼6.88 g cm^−3, similar to the densities of the Fe-Mn-Al-C type low-density steels containing 8–10 wt. % Al... Consequently, we achieve an optimized specific modulus of 32.7 GPa cm3 g^−1

claim: The addition of 4 wt.% Al stabilizes δ-ferrite, which forms elongated banded layers along the rolling direction, with higher dislocation density than austenite, indicating sluggish recrystallization during hot rolling.
evidence_text: the addition of 4 wt. % Al effectively stabilizes the ferrite and expands the ferrite-austenite region... The resultant δ-ferrite phase... leads to the banded microstructure... Within the δ-ferrite bands, many grains exhibit elongated morphologies along the rolling direction... accompanied by significantly higher dislocation densities compared to austenite... This phenomenon suggests that δ-ferrite recrystallization is sluggish during hot rolling.

claim: The TiB2 particles in the present steel are micron-scale and located primarily at grain or phase boundaries, with their size and distribution fixed by solidification, similar to conventional Fe-TiB2 steel.
evidence_text: the TiB2 particles in the present steel are micron-scale and located primarily at grain or phase boundaries, as determined by solidification. Their size and distribution are inherently fixed by the solidification path

claim: The strength-ductility combination of the present steel is comparable to that of TiB2-reinforced materials with nanoscale particle refinement, achieved through advanced processing, despite the present steel having micron-scale particles.
evidence_text: the strength-ductility combination of our material is comparable to that of TiB2-reinforced materials wherein nanoscale particle refinement is achieved via advanced processing routes... It should be emphasized that the TiB2 particles in the present steel are micron-scale

claim: The TiB2 particles in the present steel are micron-scale and located primarily at grain or phase boundaries, as determined by solidification; their size and distribution are inherently fixed by the solidification path and cannot be refined to the nanoscale through subsequent deformation.
evidence_text: the TiB2 particles in the present steel are micron-scale and located primarily at grain or phase boundaries, as determined by solidification. Their size and distribution are inherently fixed by the solidification path and cannot be refined to the nanoscale through subsequent deformation.

claim: Nanoscale TiB2 particles reported in previous studies are typically obtained through advanced, non-conventional methods such as ex-situ nano-TiB addition or ultra-fast cooling deposition.
evidence_text: nanoscale TiB2 particles reported in previous studies are typically obtained through advanced, non-conventional methods such as ex-situ nano-TiB addition or ultra-fast cooling deposition.

claim: When the applied stress is below 490 MPa, which is very close to the experimentally measured yield strength (σy), all phase stresses increase almost linearly with global true stress, indicating elastic deformation.
evidence_text: When the applied stress is below 490 MPa, which is very close to the experimentally measured σy, all the phase stresses increase almost linearly with the global true stress, indicating elastic deformation during the initial loading stage.

claim: The phase stress of δ-ferrite surpasses that of austenite by nearly 50% during initial loading, due to the high initial dislocation density and consequently high local yield strength of δ-ferrite imparted by hot rolling.
evidence_text: Note that the phase stress of δ-ferrite surpasses that of austenite by nearly 50 %. This difference arises from the high initial dislocation density and consequently, the high local yield strength of δ-ferrite due to hot rolling.

claim: The onset of yielding for the present steel is due to the plastic flow of austenite, rather than δ-ferrite, as the rate of phase stress increase for TiB2 begins to slightly decline above 490 MPa, suggesting the onset of plastic relaxation in the surrounding austenite.
evidence_text: As the global stress surpasses 490 MPa, the rate of phase stress increase for TiB2 begins to slightly decline, suggesting the onset of plastic relaxation in the surrounding austenite due to micro-yielding. ... Therefore, it is concluded that the onset of yielding for the present steel is due to the plastic flow of austenite, rather than δ-ferrite.

claim: The δ-ferrite in the present steel strain hardens at a rate that surpasses the hardening rate of α-ferrite in conventional Fe-TiB steel, likely due to the heterogeneous microstructure and contrasting phase properties that trigger additional geometrically necessary dislocations.
evidence_text: δ-ferrite in the present steel still strain hardens at an adequate rate that surpasses the hardening rate of α-ferrite in the conventional Fe-TiB steel... Such an enhanced strain hardening of δ-ferrite in the present steel is likely due to the heterogeneous microstructure (Fig. 5) and the contrasting mechanical properties between austenite and δ-ferrite, which trigger additional geometrically necessary dislocations and contribute to an extra dislocation hardening mechanism in δ-ferrite.

claim: In the homogeneous α-ferrite matrix of conventional Fe-TiB2 steel, plasticity occurs predominantly through narrow shear bands oriented ~45° to the loading axis, resulting in nearly undeformed zones that compromise strength, whereas the heterogeneous matrix of the present steel produces more uniform dislocation distributions, mitigating shear band localization and stabilizing plastic flow.
evidence_text: When considering the homogeneous matrix composed solely of α-ferrite, dislocation-mediated plasticity predominantly occurs through narrow shear bands oriented at approximately 45° to the loading axis (Fig. 12(a)). ... the emergence of shear bands can be detrimental to a material’s ductility ... By contrast, in the initially heterogeneous matrix (Fig. 12(b)), ... the heterogeneous microstructure mitigates the severe strain localization associated with shear bands, thereby stabilizing the plastic flow of the present steel with even a higher strength level.

claim: In the heterogeneous matrix of the present steel, shear bands reappear at 11% global engineering strain, corresponding to the onset of necking shown in Fig. 3(a).
evidence_text: Shear bands (orange color in Fig. 12(b)) reappear during the later deformation stages (ε 11 %) that correspond to the onset of necking in Fig. 3(a).

claim: The strengthening contribution from δ-ferrite remains almost constant at 170 MPa (>30% of the overall yield strength) throughout deformation, while the contribution from austenite increases markedly.
evidence_text: The strengthening contribution from δ-ferrite remains almost constant ( 170 MPa, which contributes to >30 % of the overall yield strength), while that from austenite obviously increases.

claim: The present steel achieves high strain hardening and good ductility through a synergy where δ-ferrite provides a constant strengthening baseline and austenite provides increasing work hardening via its remarkable dislocation activity.
evidence_text: The δ-ferrite acts as a strong, underlying framework that offers a constant base level of strength. ... This synergy between the constant strengthening from δ-ferrite and the increasing work hardening from austenite ultimately promotes the high overall strain hardening and good ductility of the present steel.

claim: At 5% global engineering strain, damage primarily arises from particle breakage; Particle 1 fractured at 4.05% global strain and Particle 2 fractured prematurely at 1.1% global strain, with the premature failure attributed to stress concentration caused by initial defects.
evidence_text: At 5 % global engineering strain (Fig. 13(a)), ... Particle 1 fractured at 4.05 % global strain, whereas Particle 2 fractured prematurely at 1.1 % global strain. The abrupt drop in σ11 stress for each particle corresponds to its fracture event. The premature failure of Particle 2 is strongly attributed to stress concentration amplification caused by its initial defects.

claim: At 7% global engineering strain, many localized deformation bands appear; agglomerated particles fracture preferentially and particle debonding occurs, with Particle 3 undergoing debonding at 5% global strain and Particle 4 experiencing two distinct fracture events.
evidence_text: At 7 % global engineering strain, both experimental and FE strain maps (Fig. 13(b1, b2)) reveal numerous localized deformation bands. ... Particle 3 exhibits debonding at 5% global strain, while Particle 4 experiences two distinct fracture events during deformation.

claim: The present low-density steel exhibits a very low density of 6.88 g/cm³, comparable to the densities of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with much higher Al additions.
evidence_text: Leveraging the beneficial lightweight effects of Al and the TiB2 particles, the present low-density steel exhibits a very low density of 6.88 g cm−3, comparable to the densities of the popular Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with much higher Al additions.
